elements.sent-2411-001: We study evolutionary equations in exponentially weighted $\Lp{2}$-spaces as introduced by Picard in 2009.
elements.sent-2411-002: First, for a given evolutionary equation, we explicitly describe the $\nu$-adjoint system, which turns out to describe a system backwards in time.
elements.sent-2411-003: We prove well-posedness for the $\nu$-adjoint system.
elements.sent-2411-004: We then apply the thus obtained duality to introduce and study notions of null-controllability for evolutionary equations.. Keywords.
elements.sent-2411-005: Evolutionary equations provide a model class for studying various partial differential equations in a unified way.
elements.sent-2411-006: Since their introduction by Rainer Picard in 2009 \cite{Picard2009 (see also \cite{PiMc11 ) quite a few particular situations have been considered in this framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Let us provide a (by no means exhaustive) list of examples.
elements.sent-2411-008: Many classical equations such as, e.g., the heat equation, the wave equation, Maxwell's equations (see \Cref{exEvoEq ), and poro- (e.g., \cite{McPic10 ) as well as fractional (e.g., \cite{PicTroWau15 ) elasticity can be modeled as evolutionary equations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: A more intricate example are certain piezoelectric coupling models.
elements.sent-2411-010: In \cite{Picard17 , they were treated with boundary dynamics using abstract boundary data spaces that do not require any regularity assumptions on the boundary (see \cite{PicTrosWau2016,PicSeiTroWau16 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, in \cite{MPTW16 thermal coupling without boundary dynamics was included, and recently in \cite{BuDo24 , thermo-piezoelectric coupling with boundary dynamics was modeled in this framework.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Another example are (infinite-dimensional) differential-algebraic equations that were also treated in the evolutionary context (e.g., \cite{TroWau2019 ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: A general overview on evolutionary equations can be found in the monographs \cite{PicardMcGheeTrostorffWaurick2020, SeTrWa22 .}

elements.sent-2411-021: As a second goal of the article, we want to study control theory for evolutionary equations.
elements.sent-2411-022: Here, we want to focus on the notion of null-controllability for evolutionary equations, which to the best of our knowledge has not been studied before.
elements.sent-2411-023: Recall that null-controllability for an inhomogeneous initial value problem with a solution existing pointwise in time means that, given a final time horizon $T$, for every initial condition we can find a suitable control function essentially acting as inhomogeneity such that the solution at time $T$ is zero.
elements.sent-2411-024: We refer to \cite{Lions1988, FursikovImanuvilov1996, TucsnakWeiss2009 for null-controllability for evolution equations.}
elements.sent-2411-025: When trying to put a corresponding definition for evolutionary equations, we have to deal with the difficulty that the solutions of evolutionary equations are only in $\Lp[\nu]{2 (\R,\HH)$, i.e., the time-regularity is only $\Lp{2}$ and we thus cannot make use of a pointwise statement on the solution at $T$.}
elements.sent-2411-026: To overcome this issue, we propose two different notions; one tailored to the $\Lp{2}$-setting and another one which can make use of pointwise statements in a weaker spatial space.
elements.sent-2411-027: In order to study null-controllability, one key method is to establish a so-called observability estimate for the dual problem, see, e.g., \cite{Douglas-66,DoleckiR-77,Lions1988,Carja1988,LebeauR-95,Zuazua-07,TenenbaumT-11,GallaunST-20,BombachGST-23 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Since we have established the duality for evolutionary equations in the first part of the article, we can now apply this duality to prove the corresponding duality statement in the context of null-controllability for evolutionary equations.
elements.sent-2411-029: We again find the well-known equivalence between null-controllability and (a version of) observability estimates for the $\nu$-adjoint system; however due to the lack of pointwise statements, the observability estimate has to be interpreted accordingly.
elements.sent-2411-030: In a special case, we also formulate a version of a pointwise interpretation of the null-controllability and pose an open problem in this situation.
